Q4 MCQ Not checked

The ground was very open and the kid saw the lioness while she was still some distance away and started bleating whereon the lioness gave up her stalk and walked straight up to it.

Correct answer: D
Solution

(d)
Stalking here refers to a silent approach of the lioness towards the 3 month old kid. She straight away walked up to the kind once the kid started crying (bleating) giving up all caution.

Solution

Q35 MSQ Not checked
Technical • COMPUTER SCIENCE • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Consider the following statements:
(a) Every candidate key of a relation is simple, then it will be always be in 2NF.
(b) If every attribute is a prime attribute in the relation then it will always be in 3NF.
(c) A prime attribute of a relation schema R is an attribute that appears in all candidate keys of R.
(d) BCNF decompositions are always lossless as well as dependency preserving.
Which of the above is/are correct?

Correct answer: A, B
Solution

(a, b)
• If every candidate key will be simple, then there will be no partial dependency, hence relation will be in 2NF. So, option (a) is correct.
• If every attribute is prime, then there will be no transitive dependency, hence the relation will always be in 3NF. So, option (b) is correct.
• A prime attribute of a relation schema R is an attribute that appears in some candidate key of R. So, option (c) is incorrect.
• BCNF decompositions are lossless but need not be dependency preserving. So, option (d) is incorrect.

S2 : Indirect addressing through register can be used to access local variables.
Which of the following option is correct?

Correct answer: A
Solution

(a)
• Relative addressing mode and based addressing modes are suitable for program reallocation at run time.
• By first loading the operand’s address in the register. Indirect addressing through register can be used to access global variables.

Q46 MCQ Not checked
Technical • COMPUTER SCIENCE • +2 / -0.66
Not attempted

If a binary search tree is not allowed to have duplicates, there is more than one way to delete a node N from the tree when that node N has two children. One way involves choosing a replacement node from the left subtree of that node N. If this is done, which node are we looking for?

Correct answer: A
Solution

(a)
When a node has two children then we can do this:
• First, we find the deletion node N (= the node that we want to delete).
• Find the predecessor node of N.
• Replace the content of node N with the content of the predecessor node.
• Delete the predecessor node.
Note: Predecessor node = the node in the left subtree that has the maximum value.

Q55 MCQ Not checked
Technical • COMPUTER SCIENCE • +2 / -0.66
Not attempted

Following is C like pseudo-code of a function that takes a queue as an argument and uses a stack S to do the processing.

What does the above function do in general?

Correct answer: D
Solution

(d)
The function takes a queue Q as an argument. It dequeues all items of Q one by one and pushes them to a stack S one after another. So, stack contains all the elements of Q but in the reverse order of that in Q i.e. Front element of Q is now the bottom element of S and rear element of Q is now top element of S. Now we pop all items of S and enqueue the items back to Q. Since stack is
LIFO order, all items of queue are reversed.

Q59 MSQ Not checked
Technical • COMPUTER SCIENCE • +2 / -0
Not attempted

Identify the correct statements:

Correct answer: B, C, D
Solution

(b, c, d)
• When we move away from the processor, access time increases. For example: Cache access time is smaller than the secondary memory.
• Temporal locality: It means that a instruction which is recently executed have high chances of execution again. This is mainly because we are executing same set of instructions again and again.
• By increasing the principal of locality of reference, throughput and speed up is improved.
• Spatial locality: It refers to the use of data elements within relatively class storage locations.

So, option (d) is also tree.
